fuzzy_prd:
We need a web request handling toolkit that lets developers build servers by composing small, reusable processing units rather than writing low-level plumbing. Both incoming requests and outgoing replies are treated as immutable data objects you can inspect and transform at any point. Each object carries header information accessible in two ways: a convenient single-value view and a full multi-value view, both sorted alphabetically when output. The system automatically determines body size from actual content, not from any manually set header. When a developer specifies a character set for a body, the content-type field is automatically rewritten to match. Bodies can only be consumed once â a second attempt triggers a well-defined error signal rather than failing silently. Incoming addresses are split into the portion already handled versus the portion yet to be routed, enabling clean nested routing without path arithmetic. Outgoing status codes come with convenience constructors for the most common outcomes â success, redirect, not-found, server error, and cache-unchanged â each populating the right default body and location information automatically. Date-related fields like freshness expiry and last-modification time are surfaced as structured timestamps. Handlers can be arranged in a fallback chain where the platform tries each one in order and skips unacceptable results. Cross-cutting concerns are layered via a wrapping mechanism where outer layers see the request first and the response last. Chunked framing is applied automatically to streaming responses that lack a declared size.

A couple extra details from the follow-up questions. If you construct a message with no body and no explicit headers, it should still report a length of 0, and the output should come through exactly as 'headers[content-length]=0' and 'headersAll[content-length]=["0"]'. More generally, headers are always emitted in ascending alphabetical (sorted) order in both views, so that applies to both headers[...] and headersAll[...]. The two header surfaces are still the same ones already described: the single-value view is singleValues (Map<String, String>) and the multi-value side comes from the base UnmodifiableMapView<String, List<String>>, exposed on Message as headers and headersAll respectively.

On routing, for a request to 'http://localhost/foo/bar', handlerPath defaults to '/' and url defaults to 'foo/bar' with no leading slash and no trailing slash. Request.handlerPath is the root-relative path to current handler and always ends with '/', and Request.url is the remaining path relative to handler plus query string. One small but important rule there: handlerPath always ends with '/' when url's path is non-empty.

A few response constructor specifics too. Response.internalServerError() with no body supplied should default body to 'Internal Server Error' and set content-type to 'text/plain', with output exactly 'status=500', 'contentType=text/plain', 'location=null', 'hasDate=false', 'body=Internal Server Error'. Response.notModified() always adds a 'date' header with the current time, so you'll see 'hasDate=true' in the output; status is 304 and body is empty. Response.found(location) sets status=302, 'location=<value>', 'contentType=null', 'hasDate=false', 'body=' and the location is taken from the argument exactly as-is.

For the date-shaped fields, parsed header values should render in ISO-8601 UTC form with milliseconds, for example '1994-11-06T08:49:37.000Z'. If the value isn't there, the output should be the literal string 'null'.

On cascading behavior, the default cascade skips handlers that return status 404 or 405 and immediately returns any other status. If all handlers return unacceptable statuses, it returns the last handler's response. Also, calling '.handler' on a Cascade with no inner handlers throws a StateError that shows up as 'error=invalid_state' with 'message=Can\'t get a handler for a cascade with no inner handlers.'. And if someone passes both statusCodes and shouldCascade to Cascade(), that should throw ArgumentError with 'error=invalid_argument' and 'message=statusCodes and shouldCascade may not both be passed.'.

For wrapping order, request phases run outermost-first and response phases run innermost-first. With two pass-through middlewares A and B around a trace handler, the trace should be exactly 'request:A', 'request:B', 'handler', 'response:B', 'response:A'. If a middleware returns a response during its request phase, the inner handler is not called and the response phases of all middlewares are skipped, so only the request trace line is emitted before the response lines.

And just to pin down the one-shot body behavior more concretely, Body.read() sets _stream = null after the first call and then throws StateError with message "The 'read' method can only be called once on a shelf.Request/shelf.Response object." on any second attempt.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: Middleware createMiddleware({FutureOr<Response?> Function(Request)? requestHandler, FutureOr<Response> Function(Response)? responseHandler, FutureOr<Response> Function(Object error, StackTrace)? errorHandler})

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the single-valued view, multiple values for the same header name are joined by a comma with no spaces. For example, values ["B1","B2"] yield 'headers[b]=B1,B2'.
A body that is a stream (including an empty stream) yields 'contentLength=null' unless a 'content-length' header is explicitly supplied. The sentinel string is exactly 'null' (not empty, not 0).
A non-identity transfer-encoding header suppresses the computed content length, yielding 'contentLength=null'. An 'identity' transfer-encoding does not suppress it.

When an explicit encoding is supplied and no content-type header is present, the media type defaults to 'application/octet-stream'. The resulting header is 'application/octet-stream; charset=iso-8859-1' (for latin1). The mimeType output is 'application/octet-stream' and encoding is 'iso-8859-1'.

Reading a body a second time emits: 'first=read', 'error=invalid_state', then 'message=The \'read\' method can only be called once on a shelf.Request/shelf.Response object.' â the exact message text including the class names in the message string must match.

Passing an empty change set returns a copy that shares the same underlying header and context instances as the original. 'headersSame=true' and 'contextSame=true'. Passing any header change (even setting a new value) sets 'headersSame=false'. Passing only a header change leaves 'contextSame=true'.

A request URI that is not absolute emits: 'error=invalid_argument', 'field=requestedUri', 'message=must be an absolute URL.' â field name is exactly 'requestedUri' and message text is exactly 'must be an absolute URL.'.
The protocol version defaults to '1.1' when not explicitly supplied. Output line: 'protocolVersion=1.1'.
A HijackException thrown from any handler always propagates untouched through all middleware layers. The top-level dispatch emits 'outcome=hijack' and no error lines.

Chunked encoding is applied only when: contentLength is null AND statusCode >= 200 AND statusCode != 204 AND statusCode != 304 AND mimeType != 'multipart/byteranges' AND the existing transfer-encoding header is null or equals 'identity' (case-insensitive). Otherwise the response is returned unchanged ('transferEncoding=null').

When chunked encoding is applied, each chunk is framed as '<hex-size>
<data>
' followed by a terminal '0

'. For bytes [104, 105] ('hi'), output is: 'transferEncoding=chunked' and 'body=2
hi
0

'.

Errors are reported as 'key=value' lines: ArgumentError emits 'error=invalid_argument', optionally 'field=<name>', optionally 'message=<text>'. StateError emits 'error=invalid_state' and 'message=<text>'. FormatException emits 'error=format'. All other errors emit 'error=failure'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) â never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only â NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic â every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handlers can be arranged in a fallback chain where the platform tries each one in order and skips unacceptable results
context_pointers.actual_meaning: golden/lib/src/cascade.dart â Cascade class with add(Handler) method and handler getter; default unacceptable statuses are 404 and 405

context_pointers.vague_reference: cross-cutting concerns are layered via a wrapping mechanism where outer layers see the request first and the response last
context_pointers.actual_meaning: golden/lib/src/middleware.dart and golden/lib/src/pipeline.dart â createMiddleware() function and Pipeline class with addMiddleware()/addHandler() chain

context_pointers.vague_reference: chunked framing is applied automatically to streaming responses that lack a declared size
context_pointers.actual_meaning: golden/lib/src/middleware/add_chunked_encoding.dart â addChunkedEncoding middleware that applies chunked transfer coding under specific conditions using chunkedCoding.encoder

oracle_data.fallback_response: This question does not match any specific documented constraint. Please consult the project specification for the relevant feature. Key principles: output is key=value lines terminated by newlines; headers are emitted in ascending sorted order; error conditions use the neutral error contract (error=invalid_argument / error=invalid_state / error=format / error=failure); the body may only be read once; streams have no known content length.

feature1_header_model.description: Construct an HTTP message from a header map whose values may be a single string or a list of strings, then inspect both header views. The single-valued view maps each name to its values joined by commas; the multi-valued view preserves the list per name. Header names are case-insensitive. A message built with no body and no explicit headers carries a default content-length of 0.

cases.expected_output:
headers[a]=A
headers[b]=B1,B2
headers[content-length]=0
headersAll[a]=["A"]
headersAll[b]=["B1","B2"]
headersAll[content-length]=["0"]

feature2_content_length.description: Determine the content length of a message from its body and headers. A string or byte body yields the byte length of the encoded body and always takes precedence over any content-length header. A body whose length cannot be determined (a stream) yields no length unless a content-length header supplies one. A non-identity transfer-encoding suppresses the computed length; an identity transfer-encoding does not.

feature3_content_negotiation.description: Derive the MIME type and character encoding of a message from its content-type header, and observe how supplying an explicit body encoding rewrites that header. The MIME type is the bare media type without parameters. The encoding comes from the charset parameter and is absent when there is no charset or the charset is unknown. When an explicit encoding is given, the charset of the content-type is set accordingly, defaulting the media type to application/octet-stream when no content-type was present.

feature4_body_read.description: Read the body of a message. Reading as text decodes the bytes using, in order of precedence, an explicit encoding argument, the content-type charset, then UTF-8. Reading as raw bytes returns the body unchanged. A string body is encoded to bytes using the message encoding. The body may be read only once; a second read fails with a neutral state error.

cases.expected_output:
first=read
error=invalid_state
message=The 'read' method can only be called once on a shelf.Request/shelf.Response object.

feature5_message_change.description: Create a copy of a message applying targeted changes. A header or context entry set to null is removed; set to an empty list is removed; set to a string or list replaces the value. New entries are added. Passing an empty change set returns the same underlying header and context instances. The body can be replaced with a new value.

cases.expected_output:
headers[content-length]=0
headers[header1]=new header value
headersAll[content-length]=["0"]
headersAll[header1]=["new header value"]
headersSame=false
contextSame=true

feature6_request_routing.description: Construct an HTTP request from a method and an absolute requested URI, and observe the routing fields. The url is the resource path relative to the current handler (plus the original query); handlerPath is the root-relative path to the current handler. They can be inferred from one another, with a trailing slash added to a non-empty handler path. Changing the path moves a leading segment from url into handlerPath. Invalid combinations and malformed arguments are reported as neutral errors. The protocol version defaults to 1.1.

cases.expected_output:
error=invalid_argument
field=requestedUri
message=must be an absolute URL.

feature7_responses.description: Construct responses using their semantic factories and observe the resulting status code, body and key headers. Success and arbitrary-status responses carry the supplied body. Redirect responses set a location header and the corresponding 3xx status. Error responses default their body and set a text/plain content-type when no body is supplied, preserving any existing content-type parameters. A not-modified response uses status 304, carries a date header and an empty body. A status code below 100 is rejected as a neutral error.

cases.expected_output:
status=500
contentType=text/plain
location=null
hasDate=false
body=Internal Server Error

feature8_date_headers.description: Parse conditional date headers into timestamps. A request exposes its if-modified-since header as a parsed UTC timestamp; a response exposes its expires and last-modified headers likewise. When the corresponding header is absent the value is null.

feature9_cascade.description: Compose several handlers into a cascade that returns the first acceptable response. By default a response is unacceptable (causing the cascade to try the next handler) when its status is 404 or 405; otherwise it is returned immediately. If all handlers are unacceptable the final response is returned. The set of cascading statuses can be replaced by an explicit list or by a predicate over the status code. An empty cascade and the simultaneous use of both a status list and a predicate are reported as neutral errors.

feature10_pipeline_middleware.description: Compose middleware around an inner handler with a pipeline. Each middleware may inspect the request before the inner handler runs and the response afterwards; request processing runs outermost-first and response processing runs innermost-first. A middleware request phase may short-circuit by returning a response, in which case the inner handler and response phase are skipped. A response phase may replace the response. An error thrown by the inner handler is offered to a middleware error phase, which may produce a replacement response or rethrow; errors from the request or response phases bypass the error phase. A hijack signal always propagates untouched.

cases.expected_output:
request:A
request:B
handler
response:B
response:A
status=200
body=ok

feature11_chunked_encoding.description: Wrap a handler with middleware that applies chunked transfer coding to streamed responses. Chunked coding is added (and the transfer-encoding header set) only when the response has no known content length, has a normal (>=200, not 204, not 304) status, is not multipart/byteranges, and does not already use a non-identity transfer-encoding. Otherwise the response passes through unchanged.
